note: Iteration 2 drills into the three T1 modules identified by Phase 0: preview-server.ts, puppeteer-renderer.ts, pdf-postprocess.ts. Only *new or refined* findings are listed. Iteration-1 findings are not re-emitted.

findings.title: Preview server path-traversal guard is symlink-unsafe — `path.resolve` does not dereference symlinks
findings.confidence_reason: POC written + executed in /tmp. `path.resolve(absDir, 'pwn')` returns a path under absDir by string, but fs.readFile follows the OS symlink and returns content from outside. Guard is bypassable.
findings.blast_radius: If any file or directory under `output/` is (or becomes) a symlink pointing outside the allowed root, the server will serve its contents to any requester. Combined with FIND-0001 (0.0.0.0 bind), this becomes remotely exploitable on a shared LAN. An attacker with local write to `output/` can plant a symlink to `/etc`, `~/.ssh`, `/home/*`, etc., then read it back over HTTP.
findings.attacker_profile: P4 (local user who writes files to output/) + P1 (LAN attacker reads). Also any code path that writes to output/ — e.g., a future template-author script that creates relative-path includes pointing outside the repo.
reproduction: # Set up the symlink (attacker primitive — file write to output/):
# POC proving path.resolve doesn't short-circuit symlinks:

recommendation.summary: Use `fs.realpath` to resolve symlinks THEN run the prefix check; or open the file with `O_NOFOLLOW` semantics (not available directly in Node, but `fs.lstat` + reject if symlink is a safe substitute for this dev tool).
recommendation.rollout_notes: Backward compatible for normal files. Legitimate symlinks in output/ (rare) will start returning 403 — add a --follow-symlinks opt-out if needed.

findings.verification_method: Create a symlink under output/ pointing to /etc; curl the server; expect 403.

findings.title: Preview server accepts URL-encoded null bytes and backslashes unfiltered
findings.confidence_reason: `decodeURIComponent(url.pathname)` will happily decode `%00` to a literal NUL. Newer Node versions throw on NUL in fs paths (`ERR_INVALID_ARG_VALUE`), so impact is typically an unhandled rejection → 500-equivalent, not a read primitive. Confidence medium because behavior depends on Node version.
findings.evidence_snippet:
const decoded = decodeURIComponent(url.pathname).replace(/^\/+/, '');
    const filePath = path.resolve(absDir, decoded);
findings.blast_radius: Uncaught exception crashes the request handler; under certain older Node versions (< 10) NULs truncated paths and were a CVE class. Modern Node throws. Low.
reproduction: Expected (post-fix): 400 Bad Request. Current: promise rejection in the handler.

recommendation.summary: Reject decoded paths containing NUL or backslash; wrap the handler in try/catch so Node version quirks don't crash the request.

findings.title: `addHyperlinks` does not validate link rect values — negative or zero-area rects are accepted
findings.confidence_reason: Read `pdf-postprocess.ts:82-107`. The 44×44 minimum enforcement only grows too-small rects; there is no check for NaN, Infinity, negative width, or negative height. A PDFHyperlink `{ rect: [0, 0, -5, 10] }` would produce an invalid /Rect that some readers reject entirely (displaying no hyperlinks at all — silent regression on link UX).
findings.blast_radius: A buggy generator (e.g., mis-computed tab coords in `daily-year-v2.ts`) passes nonsense rects silently. The PDF still "succeeds" but some readers drop all annotations on the affected page. Users report "tabs don't work" with no error from the generator.
reproduction: Open resulting PDF in a strict reader → annotations list invalid.

recommendation.summary: Validate rects at the public API boundary; reject rects whose width or height is <= 0 or non-finite; warn-and-skip instead of silently producing invalid PDFs.

findings.title: Puppeteer browser singleton race — `getBrowser()` not reentrant, concurrent calls can launch two browsers
findings.confidence_reason: Check code. Between the `if (!browserInstance)` test and the `puppeteer.launch` await, an interleaved second call sees `browserInstance === null` and launches again. Classic async check-then-act race.
findings.blast_radius: Today, `batchRenderHTML` awaits sequentially, so the race is not exercised. Any future `Promise.all([renderA, renderB])` will leak a second Chromium process. Memory waste. `closeBrowser` will only close one of them.
reproduction: Call `await Promise.all([renderHTMLToPDF(opts), renderHTMLToPDF(opts)])` — two browsers launched.

recommendation.summary: Cache the launch *promise*, not the resolved browser.

findings.verification_method: Promise.all race test; exactly one Chromium process in `ps`.

findings.title: `addBookmarks` silently clamps out-of-range `pageIndex`; masks generator bugs
findings.blast_radius: Silent clamping + warn is permissive at the boundary of a paid pipeline. A generator bug that produces bookmark.pageIndex = 999 on a 400-page PDF still ships; users see 'February' bookmark landing on the last page. `console.warn` is easily missed in batch output.
reproduction: Pass `{ pageIndex: pages.length }` — warn logged, bookmark clamps to last page.

recommendation.summary: Opinionated behavior: in strict mode, throw. In lenient mode, clamp + warn. Choose the default the CI / generator pipeline wants, and make it configurable.
recommendation.rollout_notes: Default to 'warn' for back-compat; CI should call with 'throw'.

findings.verification_method: Unit test: throw + warn paths both exercised.

findings.title: SVG sticker generators interpolate user-supplied `text` without escaping — dormant XSS primitive
findings.confidence_reason: Observed: `generateDateTab`, `generateMonthTab`, `generateDayTab`, `generateNumberCircle`, `generatePriorityMarker`, `generateBanner` all interpolate `text` directly into `<text>${text}</text>`. Today all callers pass trusted literals from enums — latent vulnerability, not currently reachable.

location.function: generateDateTab / generateMonthTab / generateDayTab / generateNumberCircle / generatePriorityMarker / generateBanner

findings.blast_radius: If a future caller passes user-controlled text (CLI flag `--sticker-text "…"`), the SVG output can contain `</text><script>…</script><text>` — breaking out of the text element. Resulting PNGs are raster-only (sharp rasterization ignores script), so immediate XSS impact = zero. But any downstream consumer that embeds the SVG in a web page would execute. Latent; worth a 2-line fix.
findings.attacker_profile: P2 future-contributor who pipes `--text` to generators.

recommendation.summary: Escape `&<>` in all interpolated `text` args before concatenation.
recommendation.rollout_notes: No behavior change for today's enum inputs.

test_to_add.name: sticker_text_is_xml_escaped
test_to_add.what_it_protects: Regression when user-controlled text is ever piped in
